The 115-page discussion paper identifies five focus sectors, proposes an AI oversight body to be hosted by NITI Aayog, and addresses four key barriers: lack of expertise, absence of enabling data ecosystems, high resource costs, and absence of formal privacy regulations. The document recommends a coordinated national approach to AI research, adoption, and international collaboration.

detail.ab: Open letter issued July 20, 2023 by CISAC and 12 other international creator/performer organizations representing over 6 million artists globally. Seven principles for policymakers: (1) creators' and performers' rights must be upheld when exploited by AI; (2) licensing solutions must be available for AI exploitation of copyright works; (3) text/data mining exceptions must not permit unauthorized exploitation; (4) credit attribution for creators whose works are exploited by AI; (5) transparency obligations for disclosure of creative works used and identification of AI-generated content; (6) legal responsibility and accountability for AI companies; (7) fully autonomous AI-generated works cannot benefit from same protection as human-created works. Co-signed by AEPO-ARTIS, ALCAM, AMA, APMA, BIEM, CIAGP, CIAM, ECSA, EVA, IMPF, MCNA, SCAPR.

detail.ab: A 33-page formal position paper submitted in response to Singapore's Bioethics Advisory Committee (BAC) consultation on 'Ethical, Legal and Social Issues Arising from Big Data and Artificial Intelligence Use in Human Biomedical Research' (May-Jul 2023). The paper is structured around two core theological-ethical principles: (1) human dignity, grounded in the Christian doctrine of Imago Dei, and (2) the common good, drawn from Scripture and the Christian social ethics tradition. It addresses: the nature of big data as a 'hyperobject'; the risk of 'Dataism' as a techno-religion; the relationship between Christian faith, science, and technology; trust in AI systems; data privacy and security (endorsing 9 data protection principles including purpose limitation, data minimization, privacy by design, right to erasure, and data breach notification); accountability and responsibility for AI errors; the adverse consequences of algorithmic bias and the need for solidarity with marginalized populations; the epistemological challenge of big data (correlation vs. causation); governance and the role of public authority; data stewardship and informed consent challenges; and the Church's role in promoting the common good. The paper argues that ethics, not technology, must be at the centre of discussions about big data and AI, and that the human person must always be front and centre in considerations about AI development and deployment. It warns against 'enthrone[ing] technology as salvific' and calls for technology to serve human dignity and the common good rather than the reverse.

detail.ab: La SNIA 2030 de la Côte d'Ivoire, officiellement validée le 13 décembre 2024 à Abidjan-Plateau, couvre la période 2024-2030. Élaborée par 60 experts sur huit mois, elle repose sur trois piliers (investissement, inclusion, gouvernance), six axes stratégiques, quinze objectifs spécifiques et 109 projets et réformes. Dotée d'un budget de 912 milliards FCFA sur cinq ans, elle prévoit la création d'un label 'Safe IA' pour encadrer légalement et éthiquement le développement des solutions IA, et la création d'une Agence de l'Intelligence Artificielle.

detail.ab: Public comment by Clare Morell and Chloe Lawrence submitted to the U.S. Department of Education via the Federal eRulemaking Portal. Addresses the Department's proposed priority on 'Advancing Artificial Intelligence in Education.' Cites research that AI in education creates 'cognitive costs,' reduces critical thinking, and undermines learning outcomes, especially for struggling students.

detail.ab: Model legislative language by Chloe Lawrence and Clare Morell to address risks posed by AI companion chatbots to children. Requires age verification for AI chatbot access, citing that 72% of American teens have used AI companions. Proposes shifting the burden of protecting children from parents to technology companies, establishing enforceable legal requirements rather than voluntary industry standards.

detail.ab: The FREE-AI Committee was constituted December 6, 2024 following RBI's Statement on Developmental and Regulatory Policies. After extensive surveys and consultations with Indian and international financial institutions and regulators, the committee released this landmark framework addressing AI risks including bias, opacity, cybersecurity vulnerabilities, and systemic financial instability. The six pillars are: Infrastructure, Policy, Capacity, Governance, Protection, and Assurance. The 26 recommendations include AI innovation sandboxes, indigenous financial AI model development, governance structures, audit mechanisms, and incident reporting. Committee includes Debjani Ghosh (former NASSCOM President) and other industry/academic leaders.
detail.ft: Framework for Responsible and Ethical Enablement of Artificial Intelligence (FREE-AI) in the Financial Sector

statement.t: RBI FREE-AI Framework

detail.ab: RCGP Council paper (September 2025) on AI in general practice affirming that AI has potential to support general practice but requires coordinated national and regional action for safe roll-out. Makes eight formal recommendations covering regulatory frameworks (urgent publication of UK AI device regulatory framework expected in 2026), national guidance, commissioner involvement, education integration, clinician design participation, supplier support, implementation timelines, and evaluation standards. Accompanied by December 2025 Nuffield Trust joint research report.

detail.ab: Adopted by the Bishops Council in Moscow (August 13-16, 2000) with 146 bishops present. The first Orthodox Church to formulate an official position on social, economic, and cultural issues. While not AI-specific, it establishes foundational principles that technologies must serve humanity, not subjugate it, and that it is unacceptable to create systems that infringe on the freedom and dignity of the personality. Covers church-state relations, human rights, mass media, healthcare, and environmental concerns. Serves as the doctrinal foundation for subsequent ROC technology positions.

detail.ab: A multidisciplinary research program at the University of Oxford founded by Nick Bostrom in 2005 focused on existential risks from advanced AI, including alignment, AI governance, and global catastrophic risk. The Institute closed on April 16, 2024.

detail.ab: Chief Abbess of Oceania Venerable Manko and Venerable Juefang addressed the relationship between AI and Humanistic Buddhism. Venerable Manko framed AI through Buddhist principles of 'faith, understanding, practice and realization,' proposing that mastering 'precepts, meditative concentration and wisdom' enables responsible technology use. The framework positions AI as a tool to facilitate compassionate action ('3 Good Deeds and 4 Givings') while cultivating a 'pure, compassionate and selfless heart.' Master Hsing Yun's theory of 'the nature of insentient beings' — that insentient beings can possess Buddha-nature — provides a philosophical foundation for Buddhist engagement with AI. Fo Guang Shan's stance: humanistic Buddhism offers moral and spiritual grounding for technological advancement rather than opposing AI development.

detail.ab: Landmark Royal Society report on machine learning technology, its promise, and societal implications. Chaired by Professor Peter Donnelly, the report called for 'careful stewardship' over the next 10 years to ensure that machine learning benefits all of UK society. Included the UK's first in-depth public survey on machine learning (with Ipsos MORI). Key recommendations: build digital skills at all levels including funded Masters and PhD programs; support machine learning research and integrate it into UK industrial strategy; establish fair data governance frameworks for secure sharing of sensitive data; foster public engagement and informed debate. Found that while only 9% of people had heard the term 'machine learning', the vast majority had used its applications.

detail.ab: UNI Global Union's foundational AI ethics document providing unions, shop stewards and workers with concrete demands regarding the transparency and application of AI as artificial intelligence, robotics, data and machine learning enter workplaces worldwide. The 10 principles operationalize the key demand that AI must put people and planet first. Key principles include: human-in-control design; worker transparency (workers must know when interacting with AI); protection against surveillance; prohibition of unfair algorithmic bias and discrimination; fair transition support through social dialogue; and mechanisms for workers to participate in AI governance. This was among the first formal trade union AI principles documents globally.

detail.ab: The first agreement of its kind between a gig worker union, traditional trade unions, a municipality, and platform companies. Signed in Bologna on May 31, 2018, following negotiations between Riders Union Bologna, Italian trade unions CGIL/CISL/UIL, the Municipality of Bologna, and platforms Sgnam and Mymenu. The Charter covers the right to be informed, the right to a fair wage, health and safety, protection of personal data, and the right to disconnect. It includes a right for trade unions to be consulted on the design of managerial algorithms and prohibits rating mechanisms based on platform worker performance. The provisions apply only to the territory of Bologna.

detail.ab: Official halakhic responsum approved by the Conservative movement's Committee on Jewish Law and Standards (CJLS reference HM 182.1.2019). Authored by Rabbi Daniel Nevins, the teshuvah surveys the field of machine ethics and brings it into conversation with halakhic discourses of agency, damages, limited liability, and ethics. Concludes that while AI and autonomous machines may assist humans in important decisions, a human must remain responsible, especially for matters of life and death, and the boundary between human and artificial life is significant and must be maintained.

detail.ab: Dubai's Islamic Affairs and Charitable Activities Department (IACAD) launched 'Virtual Ifta' at The Boulevard, Emirates Towers, on October 29, 2019. Described as the world's first AI-powered fatwa service, the system takes live questions on internet chat and replies with appropriate Islamic rulings from a pre-verified database. Initial pilot phase covers approximately 205 questions related to salah (prayer) in Arabic and English. If the AI is not 85% confident in its understanding of the question, it will not respond, instead offering similar questions or requesting rephrasing. Planned expansion to WhatsApp and additional topics including fasting, purity, ablution, and financial matters. Handles approximately 130,000 fatwa queries per year. Launched under Dubai 10X programme.

detail.ab: The AI Utilization Strategy (AI活用戦略) by Keidanren sets out business community positions on AI policy for Japan. It advocates for promoting AI adoption across all sectors to realize Society 5.0 (the concept of a human-centric, technology-integrated society), calls for appropriate data infrastructure and interoperability, and addresses workforce transformation through education and reskilling. The document emphasizes the need for flexible, innovation-enabling governance rather than restrictive regulation, and advocates international harmonization of AI governance standards. It also articulates principles for data sharing, privacy, and fairness in AI deployment.

detail.ab: Lithuania's national AI strategy, published April 2019, aims to modernize and expand the AI ecosystem with a goal of becoming a regional leader. It covers AI skills and education for all citizens, strengthening the research and innovation ecosystem, increasing AI deployment across economic sectors, promoting international collaboration, developing ethical and legal AI frameworks, and establishing a responsible data ecosystem. Priority sectors identified include manufacturing, agriculture, healthcare, and transportation.

detail.ab: The AI Utilization Guidelines (AI利活用ガイドライン) were developed by the Conference toward AI Network Society under MIC. They define ten utilization principles that AI users and operators should observe to mitigate risks and increase the benefits of AI: (1) Appropriate use, (2) Appropriate data handling, (3) Safety, (4) Security, (5) Privacy, (6) Transparency, (7) User assistance, (8) Accountability, (9) Education/Literacy, (10) Innovation. The guidelines align with the Cabinet Office's Human-Centric AI Social Principles (March 2019) and the OECD Recommendation on AI (May 2019). They are addressed to a wide range of AI users and reference hypothetical usage scenarios to facilitate practical implementation.

detail.ab: The foundational OECD Recommendation on AI, adopted at Ministerial level on 22 May 2019, providing five principles for trustworthy AI (inclusive growth, human-centred values, transparency, robustness, accountability) and five recommendations to governments on national AI policies and international co-operation. detail.ab: UNI Global Union's formal document establishing 10 operational principles for workers' data rights and protection, intended to be incorporated into collective bargaining, global agreements, and multinational alliances. The document addresses the growing use of data by management to hire, fire, promote and discipline workers. Workers are granted the right of explanation regarding what data is collected, how it is stored, its provenance, and intended use. The 10 principles are designed to address AI and algorithmic management as data-driven decision-making systems increasingly affect workers globally.

detail.ab: Official submission by the European Evangelical Alliance (representing ~23 million evangelical Christians across Europe) to the EU White Paper on AI consultation. Authored by Arie de Pater (Brussels Representative) with an interdisciplinary expert group. Asserts any use of AI should honour that humans are created in God's image. Raises concerns about AI's impact on moral autonomy, relationships, and freedom of choice. Calls for legally enforceable international oversight, comprehensive impact assessments, and inclusive societal debate including faith perspectives.

detail.ab: Adopted by the ETUC Executive Committee on 2 July 2020, this resolution responds to the European Commission's White Paper on AI and Data Strategy. The ETUC demands a legal and empowering European framework based on human rights for AI governance. Key provisions include: worker protections against workplace surveillance; classification of AI applications affecting workers' rights as high-risk systems subject to stringent regulation; trade union involvement in AI deployment decisions; and data governance frameworks that protect workers' collective interests. The resolution positioned European trade unions at the forefront of shaping the forthcoming EU AI Act.

detail.ab: Launched in January 2020 when app-based drivers from 16 countries and six continents convened in Oxford, England. The IAATW Manifesto demands the right to individual and collective control of all personal data at work, the right to transparency of algorithmic decision-making, transparency on algorithms that control earning and work including bonuses, and the right to protection of privacy at work including strict and transparent limitations on workplace surveillance. It also demands universal labor guarantees, safety protections, and the right to work for multiple companies.

detail.ab: Issued by IFLA's Freedom of Access to Information and Freedom of Expression (FAIFE) committee in October 2020. The statement outlines key considerations for the use of AI and machine learning technologies in the library sector and suggests the roles libraries should strive to take on in a society with growing AI integration. Addresses critical questions about intellectual freedom, equity and privacy, automation, the evolution of necessary digital literacy skills, and relevant Intellectual Property policy frameworks. Key principles include: AI use in libraries must be subject to clear ethical standards (per the IFLA Code of Ethics for Librarians); AI applications relying on extensive data collection must not override patron privacy choices or equity considerations; and libraries should help educate the public about AI. The statement is accompanied by three annexes covering intellectual freedom, AI literacy, and libraries at the forefront of AI.

detail.ab: Formal regulatory submission to the Privacy and Civil Liberties Oversight Board (PCLOB) opposing expansive facial recognition deployment, particularly in law enforcement and government surveillance contexts. The submission emphasizes documented accuracy and bias issues with particular disparities affecting Black Americans and communities of color, surveillance concerns regarding discriminatory targeting, and calls for robust legal constraints including requirements for transparency, accuracy testing, and clear limitations on deployment scope. Calls for moratoriums on certain applications and legislative action rather than reliance on voluntary industry standards.

detail.ab: Plum Village's Centre for Applied Ethics articulates ethical commitments for technology development rooted in the Five Mindfulness Trainings. Key principles: (1) develop technology in line with the Five Mindfulness Trainings, emphasizing healthy consumption and mindful technology use; (2) open-source code 'in the spirit of generosity and sharing'; (3) prioritize accessibility for those facing barriers due to disability, age, location, language, or financial hardship; (4) complete transparency in donations and expenses via public dashboard; (5) design for conscious consumption rather than exploitative engagement. Thich Nhat Hanh envisioned an 'Online Monastery' where people could practice mindfulness via computers, grounding digital tools in contemplative ethics.

detail.ab: The Swiss Federal Council adopted seven guidelines for AI use within the federal administration on 25 November 2020, led by the principle 'People at the Heart,' emphasizing that human dignity, fundamental rights protection, and the common good must be paramount. The guidelines serve as a binding reference for federal agencies and partners entrusted with governmental tasks, with Chapter 3 outlining ongoing monitoring provisions. Switzerland has opted for a framework approach rather than a standalone national AI strategy.

Forderungen umfassen: gesetzliche Regulierung ähnlich EU-Standards, Schutz vor KI-gestützter Arbeitsplatzüberwachung, menschliche Aufsicht, Urheberrechtsschutz für Kreative, Transparenzanforderungen und stärkere Mitbestimmungsrechte.

detail.ab: Published in the Tzu Chi Medical Journal (Vol. 32, Issue 4), this article by Prof. Michael Cheng-Tek Tai proposes four bioethics principles for AI: (1) Beneficence — AI should benefit humanity and the universe, development must be 'altruistic, not egocentric'; (2) Value-Upholding — AI must align with universal social and moral values while remaining unbiased; (3) Lucidity — AI must be transparent and comprehensible, with 'explainability and interpretability absolutely required' in high-stakes applications; (4) Accountability — developers bear responsibility for AI's societal impacts. The framework reflects Buddhist values of compassion and non-harm applied to AI governance.

detail.ab: WFEO's Committee on Engineering for Innovative Technologies (CEIT) formulated seven principles to promote responsible conduct of Big Data and Artificial Intelligence application and innovation in engineering. Released on the first World Engineering Day (March 4, 2020; announced March 16, 2020), the principles were developed through three rounds of survey consultation with more than 40 organizations and experts worldwide. The seven principles are: (1) Good for Humanity and Its Environment; (2) Inclusiveness, Fairness, Public Awareness and Empowerment; (3) Opening and Sharing while Respecting Privacy and Data Integrity; (4) Transparency; (5) Accountability; (6) Peace, Safety and Security; (7) Collaboration.

detail.ab: Coworker.org's flagship report analyzing the proliferation of employment technologies harming workers. Based on a database of over 550 labor-focused technology products, the report documents how AI, location tracking, and biometrics are used to manage employees and collect their data, often without their knowledge. Over 30% of the tracked products emerged between 2020-2021. The report and accompanying Bossware and Employment Tech Database serve as a comprehensive resource for worker advocates. A follow-up report, 'Little Tech Goes Global,' was published in June 2025 expanding analysis to Mexico, Colombia, Brazil, Nigeria, Kenya, and India.

detail.ab: Policy brief submitted to the G20 Summit in Italy, produced by the Technology and Innovation Working Group chaired by Marco Ventura. Addresses how emerging digital technologies and AI raise normative, legal, and ethical challenges requiring interfaith engagement. Argues AI should supplement, not replace, human judgment and control. Recommends human-centered AI design, protection of the most vulnerable in algorithmic decision-making, and an international dialogue framework fostering human rights. Calls for religious and belief organizations to play a constructive role in AI governance.

detail.ab: The ICRC formally recommends that states adopt legally binding international rules to regulate autonomous weapon systems (AWS) — machines that select and apply force to targets without human intervention. The position identifies three primary risks: civilian harm, IHL compliance challenges, and ethical concerns about delegating lethal decisions to machines. The ICRC proposes: (1) prohibition of unpredictable autonomous systems whose effects cannot be sufficiently understood or predicted; (2) prohibition on autonomous systems designed or used to apply force against human persons; and (3) strict regulation of all remaining AWS, constraining target types, operational duration/scope, deployment circumstances, and mandatory human-machine interaction. The ICRC calls for timely adoption of internationally agreed limits given rapid technological development, and submitted the position to the UN GGE on Lethal Autonomous Weapons Systems.

detail.ab: A formal Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct adopted by the IFIP General Assembly (September 2020, published 2021), adapted from the ACM Code of Ethics. The Code comprises three sections: seven common ethical principles shared with other professions; nine professional responsibilities specific to IT professions; and seven additional responsibilities applicable to IT professionals in leadership positions. Designed to inspire and guide the ethical conduct of all computing professionals including practitioners, instructors, and students. Also endorsed through IP3 (International Professional Practice Partnership). While not AI-specific, the Code governs conduct of computing professionals working on AI and related technologies.

detail.ab: Canada's Advisory Council on Artificial Intelligence, established in 2019, advises the federal government on building AI strengths, identifying economic opportunities, and ensuring AI reflects Canadian values. The 2020-2021 annual report documents the launch of a Public Awareness Working Group that engaged 437 Canadians across virtual workshops plus dedicated youth sessions, focusing on AI literacy and ethical AI development. The Council advises that AI governance should emphasize 'made-in-Canada' values and sustained public engagement.

detail.ab: La Quadrature du Net argues that the European Commission's proposed AI regulation, despite claiming to protect freedoms, facilitates surveillance expansion. The document proposes risk-based governance, capacity-building measures, and a framework for operationalizing ethics-by-design across public, private, and academic sectors. detail.ab: TUC's landmark AI manifesto, adopted in March 2021 following four years of research on AI in the employment relationship. The manifesto sets out fundamental values and proposals to ensure technology at work benefits everyone while preserving human agency. Key proposals include: a statutory duty to consult trade unions before deploying high-risk AI and automated decision-making (ADM) systems; right to human review and human contact when employment decisions are made by predictive AI; collective agreements reflecting manifesto values; targeting high-risk AI/ADM for enhanced regulation; and protecting equality, transparency, explainability, and work/home boundaries. The manifesto covers topics including algorithmic management, workplace surveillance, and automated disciplinary processes.

detail.ab: Tunisia's five-year AI roadmap (2021-2025) developed through a multi-ministerial MOU signed February 19, 2022, aims to secure Tunisia's place in the AI race by developing AI skills, establishing necessary infrastructure, adopting data policies, and promoting networking. Formalized through collaboration with ANPR and aligned with UNESCO AI ethics principles, it targets integration of AI in health, education, environment, and transport sectors, and laid the groundwork for the country's 2026-2030 National Development Plan which used AI tools in its drafting.

detail.ab: UNI Europa ICTS position paper on how trade unions should engage with artificial intelligence in the world of work. Addresses three key topics: data collection and management, skills and training, and Just Transition. Emphasizes that AI must enhance the quality of services from an employee-centered perspective and advocates for workers' rights, human rights, and decent working standards. Available in English, French, German, Spanish and Swedish. Developed with input from Unionen's ombudsman for digital labor markets.

detail.ab: Joint declaration between UNI Europa Finance and European insurance sector employer organizations (Insurance Europe, BIPAR, AMICE) on responsible and ethical deployment of AI. Signed after 18 months of negotiations in the presence of the European Commission. Establishes that AI should enhance rather than replace human abilities, with deployment following the 'human in control' principle. First sectoral social partner declaration to include a 'People Plan' for responsible AI throughout the value chain. Commits to transparency in AI data processing and protection against unfair bias and discrimination.

detail.ab: UNISON endorsed the TUC's 'Dignity at Work and the AI Revolution' manifesto (March 25, 2021), warning employment law is failing to keep pace with AI expansion. Key demands: legal union consultation rights before high-risk AI deployment; human review mechanisms for AI decisions; anti-discrimination safeguards in UK GDPR and Equality Act; right to disconnect. UNISON president stated reforms are urgent given increased COVID-era remote surveillance.

It urges the government to create an inter-ministerial AI Coordination Centre.

detail.ab: The Opinions establish a comprehensive governance framework for science and technology ethics in China. They designate the National Science and Technology Ethics Committee to oversee ethics governance nationally, require universities, research institutions, hospitals, and enterprises working in sensitive fields (life science, medicine, AI) to set up ethics review committees, and call for ethics legislation during the 14th Five-Year Plan period. AI is explicitly listed as one of the priority sectors requiring ethical scrutiny.

detail.ab: Sectoral social partner agreement for central government administrations on a human-centered approach to digitalisation and AI. Negotiated from January 2021 to January 2022 and signed October 6, 2022 by Ministers Petra de Sutter (Belgium) and Stanislas Guerini (France) for EUPAE, and Marco Ouwehand (FNV, Netherlands) for TUNED. Covers approximately 8 million workers and civil servants. Provides protections on telework, right to disconnect, training, health and safety, personal data protection, outsourcing, and mandates a human-in-command approach to artificial intelligence. EPSU requested the European Commission implement it through a Council decision.

detail.ab: Adopted by the ETUC Executive Committee on 6 December 2022, this resolution calls for dedicated EU legislation on algorithmic systems in the workplace. The ETUC argues that the EU AI Act is insufficient for protecting workers from the realities of algorithmic management and that a dedicated Directive is needed. Key demands include: upholding the 'human in control' principle; empowering trade unions and workers' representatives to influence AI implementation decisions; mandatory information and consultation rights before deployment of algorithmic systems; prohibition of automated dismissals; and transparency requirements for algorithmic decision-making affecting employment. The resolution was a direct precursor to ETUC's campaign for a Quality Jobs Directive.

detail.ab: Developed through tripartite global stakeholder consultation with GPAI, the Fairwork AI Principles re-assess OECD AI principles with a focus on worker welfare. Organized around five core areas — Fair Pay, Fair Conditions, Fair Contracts, Fair Management, and Fair Representation — they establish minimum fairness standards for AI in the workplace. The principles specifically address algorithmic explainability, worker appeal mechanisms, freedom of association, and democratic governance through union recognition. They were applied to rate companies like Sama and Amazon in the 2023 and 2024/25 Fairwork AI Ratings reports.

detail.ab: The Finnish Ministry of Finance sets out ethical guidelines for AI use across public administration. The guidance affirms five core principles: impartiality and independence, transparency and legitimacy, rights protection, democratic values advancement, and awareness of AI's societal impacts. It emphasises that public authorities bear full responsibility for AI systems they deploy and must not treat AI as an autonomous decision-maker.

detail.ab: Joint report by Human Rights Watch and the Harvard Law School International Human Rights Clinic. Examines why the Convention on Conventional Weapons (CCW) has failed to produce binding regulations on autonomous weapons systems despite nearly a decade of discussions, concluding that 'progress on a legally binding instrument on autonomous weapons systems is unlikely to occur under the CCW's auspices' due to consensus-based decision-making, lack of shared purpose, and absence of deadlines. Proposes that proponents of regulation pursue alternative treaty-making processes modeled on the Ottawa Process (Mine Ban Treaty, 1997), Oslo Process (Convention on Cluster Munitions, 2008), and UN General Assembly approach (Treaty on Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons, 2017). Recommends an independent or UNGA-initiated negotiating process featuring majority-vote decisions, ambitious timelines, and broad civil society participation. HRW co-founded the Stop Killer Robots campaign comprising 270+ NGOs across 70 countries.

Seven chapters cover: basic research and technology development, industrial infrastructure, application expansion, promotion and assurance, governance principles and measures. detail.ab: Transparency International working paper introducing the concept of 'corrupt AI' — the intentional abuse of AI systems by those in positions of public or private power for personal gain. The paper argues that while AI is increasingly promoted as an anti-corruption tool, its potential as a new enabler of corruption has received insufficient attention. Power in the digital age concentrates with those controlling algorithms, typically tech companies and governments, creating corruption risks when these actors abuse this power for private gain through designing, manipulating, or deploying AI systems. Recommends: developing regulatory frameworks mandating ethical AI design and model audits; ensuring transparent code, data, and interoperability; and training AI developers in ethics and anti-corruption through codes of conduct.

Joint research between Theos and the Faraday Institute, funded by the Templeton Religion Trust.

detail.ab: Briefing paper by Hannah Waite and Nick Spencer exploring whether robots could one day have souls or rights. Based on a YouGov survey of 5,000+ UK adults and over 100 in-depth interviews with leading scientific and religious thinkers. Finds that 75% of people are not convinced robots are 'just like us' and do not believe they have souls, though 27% of under-30s think robots will one day have human rights. Joint research between Theos and the Faraday Institute, funded by the Templeton Religion Trust.

detail.ab: Congress motion noting increased technology use in post-16 education during the pandemic and expanded employer data collection on members. Notes that Data Protection Impact Assessments should be carried out when implementing new systems using workers' personal data but unions are not consulted. Believes AI can be useful but must not infringe on workers' rights or discriminate against individuals and must be fully accessible. Resolves to: (a) set up an expert group on AI and datafication to identify threats and opportunities and make recommendations on UCU AI policy; (b) include in national claims an element on agreeing an ethical AI policy with employers establishing a consistent approach across sectors; (c) establish the role of Data Representative in each branch.

detail.ab: Published by Worker Info Exchange, this report documents harms caused by gig platforms' algorithmic workforce management systems. Based on over 500 subject access requests on behalf of workers at Amazon Flex, Bolt, Deliveroo, Free Now, Just Eat, Ola, and Uber, it finds woefully inadequate transparency of algorithmic management systems, questionable predictive tools used to predict 'fraudulent behaviour' of workers, and disproportionate surveillance resulting in unaccountable expansion of law enforcement access to personal data. The report was co-authored by researcher Cansu Safak and former Uber driver James Farrer.

detail.ab: AAFP policy stating that the family medicine experience is rooted in a deeply personal physician-patient interaction supported by technology. As AI expands the care triad, the patient-physician relationship must be preserved and ideally enhanced. AI/ML solutions must adhere to principles including transparency around training data and model decisions, processes to identify and mitigate algorithmic bias, training data diversity, data privacy with explicit consent, user-centered design, rigorous evaluation comparable to other clinical tools, and trustworthy systems design. AAFP supports risk-based liability frameworks and requires companies providing AI solutions to disclose how their models make predictions.

detail.ab: A technical policy brief examining the societal risks of rapidly commercialized generative AI technologies, including misinformation, cyberattacks, and environmental harms. Recommends end-to-end governance addressing risks by design across the full development-to-deployment lifecycle, comprehensive regulation of foundation models and their supply chains, and legal liability for irresponsible practices. Co-authored by David Leslie (Alan Turing Institute) and Francesca Rossi (IBM AI Ethics). NOTE: This is distinct from the 2023 ACM Principles for Generative AI (STMT-0329), which is already in vault.

detail.ab: Formal 11-page submission dated September 8, 2023 to Canada's Standing Committee on Industry and Technology's Artificial Intelligence and Data Act consultation. Core principles: consent, compensation, and control for performers. Key demands: new AI legislation must allow performers to consent to and control the use of their voice, image, or likeness by AI; compensation must be provided for such use; AI-generated material should never be considered Canadian content. 98% of surveyed members concerned about misuse of their name/image/likeness by AI; 93% concerned AI will eventually replace human actors.

detail.ab: Open letter signed by 20 faith and civil society leaders addressed to Secretary of State Michelle Donelan ahead of the Bletchley Park AI Safety Summit. Highlights six priorities: accountability, accessibility of progress, ethical guidelines, short-term harm prevention, diverse voices, and closing the AI literacy gap. Chaired by Rt Hon Sajid Javid MP at inaugural high-level meeting. Commissioners include Dr Omar Al Derei, Shaykh Abdullah Bin Bayyah, Father Paolo Benanti (Pontifical Academy for Life), Rev Dr Kathryn Pritchard (Lambeth Palace), and Rabbi Dr Harris Bor. Advocates five core values: Truth, Transparency, Justice, Trust, and Accessibility.

detail.ab: Formal brief submitted by the Assembly of First Nations to the Canadian Parliamentary Standing Committee on Industry and Technology regarding Bill C-27 (Digital Charter Implementation Act, 2022). AFN argues that the Artificial Intelligence and Data Act (AIDA) and the Voluntary Code of Conduct Relating to Advanced Generative AI Systems do little to assure First Nations that their individual and collective rights will be respected. The brief asserts First Nations hold an inherent right to data sovereignty essential to self-determination and self-government, and criticizes the lack of Nation-to-Nation consultation in developing the legislation. AFN warns that Bill C-27's Consumer Privacy Protection Act does not account for collective rights harm from de-identification and aggregation of First Nations data, and that the legislation infringes First Nations rights to self-government. Calls for amendments including non-derogation clauses, duty to consult, and constraints on government authority to develop and use AI affecting First Nations.

detail.ab: Adopted by the AFT Executive Council on June 1, 2023 in Washington, D.C. The resolution calls for social media, AI, and machine learning models to be developed and employed ethically, with developers held accountable for real-world harms. It urges governments to implement strict regulations protecting privacy and security, demands equity and social accountability from AI and social media platforms, and calls for worker protections against AI-driven job displacement across education, healthcare, and public services. The AFT specifically opposes using AI to undermine collective bargaining and calls for transparency in technology deployment. The resolution acknowledges both the transformative potential and significant risks of rapid AI advances including ChatGPT, highlighting concerns about misinformation, bias, and privacy violations.

detail.ab: Published May 2023, setting out the Authors Guild's formal policy proposals on generative AI. Key demands: Consent (require permission for use of writers' works in AI training), Compensation (ensure authors are paid when works are used), Transparency (require AI developers to disclose training data), Use in outputs (require permission when works/names/identities appear in outputs), and Labeling (require AI-generated works to be labeled). Advocates for collective licensing as the mechanism for AI training data. The Guild represents over 13,000 professional authors and has since pursued class-action litigation against OpenAI, Microsoft, and Anthropic.

detail.ab: The 2023 AI Now Landscape Report frames the central challenge of AI as one of power concentration. Authored by Amba Kak and Sarah Myers West, the report provides strategic analysis of AI governance challenges and presents structural policy recommendations for limiting tech company power, identifies ineffective popular policy approaches, and recognizes nonregulatory interventions including worker organizing and collective action. The objective is ensuring technology serves public rather than corporate interests.

detail.ab: An ASA board statement adopted August 4, 2023, revising and expanding the 2015 ASA Statement on the Role of Statistics in Data Science to explicitly incorporate artificial intelligence. The statement articulates the essential contributions of statisticians to AI and data science endeavors, emphasizing that successful AI requires statistical expertise in uncertainty quantification, sampling design, and causal inference, and that data science and AI require multifaceted collaboration. Notes that the 'big tent of statistics has grown massively bigger' and that statisticians must adapt their roles to this evolving landscape.

detail.ab: Examines how generative AI affects election infrastructure security and public confidence in elections. Identifies four primary threat vectors: enhanced phishing attacks, advanced malware, deepfakes and website impersonation, and weaponized FOIA requests. Recommends strengthening cybersecurity infrastructure, providing technical support to under-resourced local jurisdictions, authenticating election communications, training election workers, and countering false narratives. Authored by Lawrence Norden and Gowri Ramachandran.

detail.ab: Examines how AI is currently deployed in election administration and recommends safeguards for its expanded use. Presents the 'AI CPR' framework: Choose systems carefully (prioritizing simplicity and human oversight), Plan thoughtfully (including staff training and contingency procedures), and Review thoroughly (assessing performance and ensuring human involvement in consequential decisions). Addresses risks including algorithmic bias, hallucinations in generative systems, and potential voter disenfranchisement. Authors: Edgardo Cortés, Lawrence Norden, Heather Frase, Mia Hoffmann.

detail.ab: The Canadian Bar Association's submission on international negotiations for a Council of Europe treaty on artificial intelligence, human rights, and the rule of law. Developed jointly by CBA sections on Privacy and Access Law, Immigration Law, and Ethics and Professional Responsibility, the submission supports the treaty approach and addresses application to public and private sectors, protection of vulnerable populations including migrants, algorithmic transparency, facial recognition and biometric surveillance risks, deep fake technology, civil and criminal liability frameworks, and digital profiling. The CBA advocates for regulatory frameworks that harness AI's potential while minimizing risks.

detail.ab: The CCBE's formal statement on the use of AI in the justice system and law enforcement acknowledges the potential benefits of AI tools while highlighting the need for careful consideration of risks to human rights and the rule of law. The statement calls for AI use to fully respect fundamental rights including privacy, freedom of expression, non-discrimination, and the right to a fair trial. It calls for a ban on predictive and profiling AI tools in law enforcement and criminal justice, asserts that judicial decisions must remain human-driven, and demands that AI systems not undermine lawyer-client confidentiality. The CCBE calls for defined requirements including transparency, non-discrimination, and protection of the presumption of innocence before any AI tools are deployed in justice systems.

Investigates six themes: inequality in access to high quality healthcare, transparency, social bias in AI systems, dilution of the patient's account of well-being, automation bias and displaced liability, and the right to privacy. detail.ab: Chief Rabbi Eliezer Simha Weisz, member of the Council of the Chief Rabbinate of Israel, signed the Rome Call for AI Ethics at a Vatican ceremony on January 10, 2023, alongside representatives of Christianity and Islam. The Rome Call, originally established in February 2020, urges governments, technology companies, and organizations to advance an ethical approach to AI that honors human dignity. The Chief Rabbinate's Commission for Interfaith Relations co-promoted the event. Rabbi Weisz stated that 'Judaism extols the wisdom of humanity, created in the Divine Image of God manifest generally in human innovation and in Artificial Intelligence in particular.' This represents the first formal Jewish institutional commitment to a major international AI ethics framework.

detail.ab: Organizational guidance on AI published through Cru's Digital Academy as part of 'The Digital Download' series. Features perspectives from four Cru leaders including Cheryl Boyd (Global VP of Digital Strategies) and Andre Echevarria (US Digital Strategies leader). Outlines three core pillars: pray for wisdom, seek understanding, look at the fruit. Includes two formal guidance documents: one for appropriate AI uses in ministry and an ethical framework with six principles. States AI is 'neither good nor bad' but use must promote deeper relationships rather than isolation. Policy mandates that fully automated virtual content is never used. Positions AI as an 'advanced intern' requiring human oversight.

The handbook aligns with India's emerging data protection regulatory landscape (DPDP Act) and globally recognized ethical frameworks. Produced through a collaboration between DSCI (NASSCOM-promoted industry body), Germany's GIZ (development agency), Digital India Foundation, and Koan Advisory Group.

detail.ab: Engineers Australia's formal submission to the Australian Government's 'Safe and Responsible AI in Australia' discussion paper (July 2023). The document articulates Engineers Australia's position that engineers have a professional responsibility to navigate the ethical and technical considerations of AI to harness its potential while ensuring the safety and wellbeing of society. It characterizes AI from an engineering perspective as an engineered system with defined inputs, outputs, and operations, emphasizing that AI is fundamentally deterministic in behavior and does not have intent independent of programming. Advocates for robust governance frameworks, engineering oversight, and ethical guardrails.

detail.ab: Launched mid-2023, Equity's comprehensive AI toolkit was developed in partnership with IP expert Mathilde Pavis to protect performers from unregulated AI. Includes: a vision statement on ethical AI applications, template AI contract for performance cloning work, model AI clauses to prevent unauthorized cloning, and template take-down notices. Core principles: explicit consent, transparency of terms, and fair remuneration for usage. Equity argues the UK legal framework is inadequate to protect performers from unauthorized AI imitations. In 2025, 99% of 7,000 members voting supported refusing digital scans to secure AI protections, and Equity escalated to industrial action threat.

detail.ab: The FBE New Technologies Commission's guidelines on the responsible use of generative AI by European lawyers, providing seven core recommendations: (1) understand the technology, (2) acknowledge limitations and context, (3) adhere to existing rules on AI use, (4) complement legal expertise, (5) maintain attorney-client privilege, (6) ensure data protection and privacy, and (7) inform clients and assume responsibility. The guidelines affirm that legal decisions must remain under human control and that AI serves as a tool supporting — but never replacing — professional experience and critical judgment. Translated into Italian, Spanish, Polish, German, and Turkish. Superseded by the 2024 Guidelines 2.0.

detail.ab: Published 17 November 2023 (updated March 2025), this guide helps FIA affiliates understand AI's implications for performers. It outlines FIA's core principles for responsible AI deployment: informed consent for digital replicas of performers' voices and likenesses, fair compensation models, transparency of training datasets, and protection of performers' biometric data. The guide provides practical bargaining strategies for trade unions to enhance protections through collective agreements and advocates for regulatory frameworks centered on consent and compensation.

detail.ab: Issued 9 February 2023 by six European authors' and performers' organisations (ECSA, EWC, FERA, FIA, FIM, FSE), this joint statement addresses the EU AI Act. It advocates for a human-centric approach based on fundamental rights including artistic freedom and property rights. The statement demands mandatory consent before works are used to train AI, full transparency and disclosure requirements covering all deepfakes without creative-work exceptions, and protection of professional creators from unauthorized AI misappropriation of their economic interests and attribution rights.

detail.ab: Research project examining how the world's major traditional religions view the development and governance of artificial intelligence, and what positive AI futures look like through the lens of diverse faith traditions. Notes that most of the global population participates in a traditional religion, yet religious perspectives are largely absent from strategic AI discussions. Explores how Buddhism's mindfulness and compassion, Islam's justice and ethical values via Sharia, Christianity's emphasis on human dignity and stewardship, and other traditions approach AI technology.

detail.ab: Free Press report by Nora Benavidez examining how Meta, Twitter, and YouTube are failing to protect democracy ahead of the 2024 U.S. elections. Documents elimination of 17 critical platform policies between November 2022 and November 2023, including rollbacks of election-misinformation policies and weakening of protections around political ads. Addresses AI-generated deepfakes as a specific emerging threat to electoral integrity. Notes collective layoffs of more than 40,000 employees in content-moderation and trust-and-safety roles. Calls for reinstatement of platform safeguards and regulatory action on AI-generated disinformation.

detail.ab: Published September 2023 (also issued as statement of concern to IFRRO General Assembly). Position grounded on three fundamental rights of artists: credit, consent, and compensation. Key positions: works created solely via AI without human-created input do not constitute works of human authorship; any use of copyrighted works for AI/ML training without permission is copyright infringement; the primacy of human endeavor must be recognized; creative professions and livable income must be protected. Submitted formal comments to U.S. Copyright Office in October 2023 arguing AI image generation is not fair use. Also joined the Human Artistry Campaign. Advocates for collective licensing as remuneration mechanism.

detail.ab: The German Ethics Council explores how AI reshapes human identity and relationships, examining philosophical foundations and evaluating whether AI expands or diminishes human authorship and responsible action. The opinion covers four domains—medicine, education, public communication, and public administration—and concludes that AI must foster human flourishing rather than replace human judgment or responsibility.

detail.ab: Launched March 16, 2023 by a coalition of 40+ organizations spanning music, film, TV, publishing, and visual arts. Sets out seven core principles for AI that emphasize: technology should empower human expression; human-created works remain essential; use of copyrighted works requires authorization and licensing; AI must not receive copyright exemptions; copyright protects human creativity not machine output; transparency and accountability in AI development; and creators must maintain control. Co-signatories include RIAA, Recording Academy, SAG-AFTRA, AFM, NMPA, Nashville Songwriters Association, Graphic Artists Guild, and many others. One of the most broadly endorsed multi-union creative AI statements globally.

detail.ab: Human Rights Watch joined 149 civil society organizations urging EU institutions to prioritize fundamental rights during AI Act negotiations. Calls for: mandatory human rights impact assessments during AI development and deployment; public transparency requirements; prohibitions on harmful surveillance technologies including real-time biometric identification, predictive policing systems, and emotion recognition tools in public spaces; bans on discriminatory AI applications; and effective remedies for people harmed by AI systems. States that 'companies and governments will continue to use AI systems that exacerbate mass surveillance' without strong regulation.

detail.ab: Eight core principles adopted by IATSE addressing AI and machine learning in the entertainment industry, developed by the union's Commission on Artificial Intelligence established in May 2023. The principles cover: (1) Comprehensive Approach spanning research through collective bargaining; (2) Research into AI/ML capabilities and impacts; (3) Collaboration with partners and stakeholders including AFL-CIO and international labor groups; (4) Education and upskilling rights for members; (5) Organizing entertainment workers in new AI-related job classifications; (6) Maintaining workers' rights, job security, and union jurisdiction, with AI serving as a complementary tool rather than replacement; (7) Political and legislative advocacy for strong copyright and IP protections; and (8) Collective bargaining, declaring AI a topic for future contract negotiations with demands for employer transparency.

detail.ab: Released by the Department of Health Research and ICMR's Artificial Intelligence Cell, these guidelines provide India's first comprehensive ethics framework for AI in biomedical research and healthcare. The document includes an ethics review process for AI-based medical interventions, governance structures for AI use, informed consent requirements for AI-involved care, and special protections for sensitive health data. Target audience includes AI creators, researchers, clinicians, ethics committees, sponsors, and funders. Guidance addresses fairness in training datasets, privacy-by-design, and psychological/social risk communication.

detail.ab: Final Pronouncement issued April 11, 2023 by the International Ethics Standards Board for Accountants (IESBA), an independent standard-setting board under the auspices of IFAC. Introduces technology-related revisions to the International Code of Ethics for Professional Accountants, addressing the ethical responsibilities of accountants and auditors when using or advising on technology including AI. Available in multiple languages. Related companion documents include the IESBA Technology Working Group Phase 2 Report (November 2022) and guidance on Applying the Code's Conceptual Framework to Independence in technology-related situations (July 2023). These represent the most formally binding AI-related guidance in the IFAC ecosystem, as IESBA standards are adopted by member bodies worldwide.

detail.ab: Unanimously adopted at the IPI 72nd Annual General Assembly during the IPI World Congress in Vienna, Austria, May 2023. The resolution calls on governments to ensure AI development includes 'strong human rights safeguards that protect media freedom and enable independent, pluralistic information ecosystems.' Main concerns addressed: AI's potential to spread disinformation and harmful content; risk of AI-enabled surveillance and tracking of journalists; need for human rights-based governance of AI systems. The resolution acknowledges AI can help media organizations become more resilient and sustainable through improved newsgathering and storytelling, but warns of risks including AI-generated disinformation and deepfakes. It calls on states to consult 'a diverse spectrum of civil society stakeholders' including journalists and media professionals. The document references the EU AI Act, OSCE Policy Manual on Artificial Intelligence and Freedom of Expression, and Council of Europe recommendations as relevant frameworks.

detail.ab: ISACA white paper examining how organizations should navigate AI adoption while managing associated risks. Provides a framework for enterprise AI governance covering benefits analysis, risk identification (IP leakage, data integrity, cybersecurity threats, liability), continuous risk management via a three-step approach (identify risk, define appetite, monitor/manage), and eight security protocols. Targeted at CISOs, IT risk managers, and senior executives. Includes guidance on establishing acceptable use policies, designating an AI governance leader, and building AI ethics programs. Formally issued by ISACA as an institutional guidance document.

detail.ab: Formal institutional policy governing Brookings staff use of generative AI in research and operations. Developed through a deliberate process launched by Brookings Interim President Amy Liu and executive leadership via the Emerging Technologies Advisory Group (ETAG). Establishes four core institutional principles: complying with existing policies, validating AI outputs, protecting sensitive data, and disclosing AI usage appropriately. Described as 'the first step' in a comprehensive institutional AI strategy. detail.ab: A research report commissioned by the ITUC and produced by AlgorithmWatch, presenting findings of a global mapping exercise on trade union responses to algorithmic management across 26 countries. The report finds that workers worldwide are confronting algorithmically-driven automation through automated workforce management procedures, leading to new forms of workplace surveillance that potentially undermine workers' rights. Key findings include: transparency and accountability are critical tools against automation risks; unions have primarily focused on establishing ethical frameworks rather than practical implementation; and inter-union knowledge exchange is underdeveloped. The report calls for unions to transition from principles to practical implementation through concrete tools at company level.
detail.ft: Algorithms and Automation in the Workplace: A Global Mapping of Trade Union Responses

Guided by six regulatory principles including international interoperability, incremental development, and multistakeholder cooperation, it addresses seven AI challenges including algorithmic bias, lack of transparency, safety risks, and accountability gaps, and proposes an AI Policy Coordination Center for inter-agency coordination.

detail.ab: The AI Utilization Strategy II (AI活用戦略II) is Keidanren's updated policy proposal addressing the rapidly evolving AI landscape four years after the original 2019 strategy. The document responds specifically to the rise of generative AI (e.g., large language models) and sets out guidance across three domains: (1) preparation by companies to deploy AI responsibly, (2) preparation by individuals to work with AI, and (3) creation of social systems and governance frameworks enabling safe AI diffusion. It endorses the Hiroshima AI Process principles and calls for international coordination on AI standards, urging the Japanese government to adopt risk-based, innovation-friendly regulation.

detail.ab: The Law Council of Australia's submission to the Department of Industry, Sciences and Resources in response to the government's discussion paper on Safe and Responsible AI in Australia. The Law Council argues that the significant risks posed by AI use justify a strengthened, precautionary approach to regulation where existing laws prove insufficient. It advocates for multifaceted regulation including targeted legislation beyond voluntary codes, while opposing a comprehensive 'AI Act' as the primary mechanism. Key recommendations include product stewardship across the supply chain, transparency and disclosure obligations, and expanded regulation in high-risk sectors.

detail.ab: The Provisional Guidelines on Generative AI in Primary and Secondary Education (初等中等教育段階における生成AIの利用に関する暫定的なガイドライン) were issued by MEXT's Elementary and Secondary Education Bureau in July 2023 as an urgent response to the rapid spread of tools such as ChatGPT among students. The guidelines define (1) general principles for school-level AI use, (2) specific situations where use is appropriate vs. inappropriate (e.g., inappropriate for assessments measuring individual ability), (3) countermeasures for risks including privacy, copyright, misinformation, and over-reliance, and (4) guidance on fostering digital and AI literacy. They target school boards, school principals, and teachers. A revised Version 2.0 was issued December 26, 2024, with an updated English translation released April 22, 2025.

detail.ab: Described as a first-of-its-kind industry-led self-governance framework globally, these guidelines divide obligations across three actor categories — researchers, developers, and users — and establish joint obligations including AI literacy programs, technical safety research, and regulatory reform projects. Developers must employ ethics councils and risk officers, publicly disclose data and algorithm sources, and ensure responsible use terms of service. The guidelines target risks specific to generative AI including deepfakes, hallucinations, and job displacement.

detail.ab: The Global Principles on Artificial Intelligence (August 2023) were co-signed by 26 international publisher organizations representing thousands of creative professionals across news, magazine, and book publishing. The document sets out 12 numbered principles governing AI development, deployment, and regulation: (1-4) Intellectual Property — AI developers must respect IP rights; publishers are entitled to negotiate remuneration for use of their IP; copyright applies to AI training use; existing licensing frameworks should be used; (5) Transparency — AI systems must keep granular records of publisher works used in training, with full provenance chains; (6) Accountability — providers must cooperate to ensure accountability for outputs and must not be shielded by safe harbor provisions; (7) Quality and Integrity — quality publisher content upstream ensures quality AI outputs downstream; (8) Fairness — AI systems must not create anti-competitive market outcomes; (9-10) Safety — AI must promote trusted information sources and must protect user privacy; (11) By Design — these principles must be incorporated at design stage, not as afterthought; (12) Sustainable Development — AI systems must align with human values and support long-term information ecosystems. Signatories include WAN-IFRA, News/Media Alliance, European Newspaper Publishers' Association, News Media Association (UK), Digital Content Next, European Publishers Council, FIPP, Korean Association of Newspapers, and 18 other national and regional publisher bodies.

detail.ab: The NMA AI Principles (April 2023) articulate the organization's foundational position that unlicensed use of journalistic content by generative AI systems constitutes intellectual property infringement. The document covers six categories: (1) Intellectual Property — GAI developers must respect copyright and seek explicit permission for training, surfacing, and synthesizing publisher content; publishers have the right to negotiate fair compensation; fair use doctrine does not justify unauthorized AI use of publisher archives; (2) Transparency — publishers have the right to know what content is used in AI systems; surfaced and synthesized outputs must attribute original sources clearly; (3) Accountability — deployers of GAI systems must not be shielded from liability for outputs; (4) Fairness — GAI systems must comply with competition laws; unauthorized use of publisher content by firms with market power should be considered evidence of competition law violation; (5) Safety — GAI systems must protect user privacy; collection of personal data should be minimal and disclosed; (6) Design — all principles should be incorporated by design into GAI systems. The document explicitly states these principles 'apply equally to all publisher content, whether in text, image, audiovisual or any other format.'

detail.ab: Member-led AI policy paper endorsed by NTEU National Council in October 2023. Developed through a working group of NTEU members formed in 2023 with widespread consultation and focus groups. Recognises AI's potential to enhance and enrich work in the tertiary sector provided ethical implementation. Key positions: staff are best positioned to make decisions about AI integration in their discipline's learning context; access to and training on AI should be provided at institutional level to ensure equity; protection of intellectual property and author moral rights; need for strong guardrails beyond Fair Work Act provisions; collaborative design processes rather than post-decision consultation. Also informed NTEU's submission to government inquiries on AI regulation.

detail.ab: NU's National Assembly of Religious Scholars (Musyawarah Nasional Alim Ulama) established that it is haram (forbidden) to use AI-generated answers as authoritative religious guidance. AI lacks the qualifications required of a mufti including piety, scholarly depth, and contextual understanding. AI may inform by providing information about what scholars have said, but must not rule by issuing its own religious judgments. This decision forms the basis for the subsequent SOUL.md AI ethics framework.

detail.ab: Formally adopted and ratified October 30, 2023 after drafting over summer/fall 2023. Establishes six core principles: Solidarity, Humanity, Control and Compensation, Transparency, Accountability, and Integrity. Outlines five key policy areas: Control, Compensation, Credit/Labeling/Transparency, Fair Contracts, and Compliance with the Berne Convention and other copyright treaties. Emphasizes that creators should never be replaced by generative AI and that if AI is used, it must serve as a tool to assist human workers. Also submitted response to U.S. Copyright Office NOI on same date. The NWU is UAW Local 1981, representing freelance and contract writers.

OTI framed the legislation as an important step in holding companies accountable for automated decision-making in critical areas like housing, employment, and education, while balancing innovation protections.

detail.ab: Panoptykon Foundation examines the rapid rise of generative AI tools like ChatGPT and their societal implications, identifying concerns including legal liability for AI-generated content, algorithmic bias, privacy erosion, and disinformation. The paper argues that existing regulations (GDPR, DSA/DMA) have gaps in AI governance, and advocates for EU AI Act amendments requiring fundamental rights impact assessments and mandatory labeling of AI-based systems.

detail.ab: Joint interfaith statement issued in the context of the UN CCW expert meeting (12–19 May 2023, Geneva) that failed to achieve legally binding controls on lethal autonomous weapons systems. Pax Christi International joined 14 other Christian, Buddhist, and Muslim organizations calling for a ban on autonomous weapons that target people or cannot be used with meaningful human control. The statement condemns 'digital dehumanization' — the reduction of humans to data points for automated attacks — as 'fundamentally repulsive to all people who share a belief in the inalienable dignity of the human person and the inestimable worth of human life.' Affirms that machines will never have human consciousness or awareness. All 15 signatories: Aotearoa New Zealand Stop Killer Robots Campaign, Christian World Service (NZ), Fellowship of Reconciliation (England and Scotland), Hungary Stop Killer Robots Campaign, Institute of Church and Society (Ibadan, Nigeria), Kairos Foundation of Nigeria, New Zealand Anglican Pacifist Fellowship, Pax Christi Aotearoa New Zealand, Pax Christi International, Peace Movement Aotearoa NZ, Soka Gakkai International, St Andrew's on the Terrace Presbyterian Church (Wellington NZ), Sustainable Peace and Development Organization (SPADO), The InterAgency Institute, World Council of Churches.

Covers five issue areas: non-consensual intimate deepfakes, election deepfakes, chatbot disclosure requirements, AI in healthcare coverage decisions, and chatbot safety for minors. Offers expert support for state lawmakers to tailor models to specific state needs. Part of Public Citizen's broader effort to develop common-sense legislative solutions to mitigate the most dangerous harms of unregulated AI in the absence of federal action.

detail.ab: Clerk's reflection by Gray Cox calling Quakers to engage with AI ethics. Identifies concerns about opacity of black-box AI systems, speed outpacing oversight, training data absorbing unethical content, and documented harms (racial discrimination in lending, facial recognition bias, democratic interference). Calls for slowing AI development, directing technology toward ecologically balanced 'Collaborative Wisdom,' engaging Quaker communities in spiritual discernment, and transforming systems through spirit-led approaches. Proposes workshops examining AI from Quaker perspectives.

detail.ab: Ayatollah Alireza Arafi, head of Qom's seminaries, stated: 'The seminary must get involved in using modern, progressive technology and artificial intelligence.' Supreme Leader Ayatollah Khamenei urged clergy to explore AI. Qom seminaries aim to use AI to parse Islamic texts faster and help fatwas keep pace with evolving society. Mohammad Ghotbi noted: 'Robots can't replace senior clerics, but they can be a trusted assistant that can help them issue a fatwa in five hours instead of 50 days.' AI positioned as assistant to human jurists, not replacement.

detail.ab: RTDNA recommends that news organizations establish clear policies for AI use in newsgathering, editing, and content distribution. The guidelines address three primary areas: (1) Accuracy, Context and Clarity — newsrooms should verify AI capabilities before implementation, establish safeguards against unintended plagiarism, and mandate human review before publication; (2) Transparency and Disclosure — 'disclosing how you use artificial intelligence is preferable to non-disclosure,' with organizations required to define what constitutes AI use and where disclosure occurs; (3) Privacy — AI should operate within ethical and legal boundaries, as it cannot properly weigh public interest against individual privacy rights in the way human journalists do. The overarching principle is that 'AI should not be used to replace human judgment and critical thinking — essential elements of trusted reporting.' The document acknowledges this is 'an emerging and fast-changing area' requiring periodic review.

Chaired by journalist and Nobel laureate Maria Ressa; co-signed by 16 partner organizations.

detail.ab: SAG-AFTRA's formal position statement asserts that terms for digitally simulating performers' voices and likenesses are mandatory subjects of bargaining under the National Labor Relations Act, requiring companies to bargain with the union before acquiring these rights. The statement reaffirms Global Rule One, opposes circumvention of collective bargaining via individual contracts, supports the Human Artistry Campaign's AI principles, and advocates against government creation of IP exemptions that would allow AI developers to exploit creative works without permission or compensation.

detail.ab: The Southern Baptist Convention became the first major national denomination to pass a formal resolution on AI ethics, adopted at its Annual Meeting in New Orleans. The resolution affirms human dignity as image-bearers of God over productivity, calls for proactive engagement with AI rather than fear or uncritical embrace, and directs Christ-centered, transparent use of AI that prioritizes human uniqueness.

detail.ab: The New Square Beis Din, signed by 16 rabbinic members, issued a proclamation banning the use of 'Open AI and the like' in all forms — via computer, text, or phone. The decree characterizes AI as 'an open internet without any filter, open to all abominations, heresy, and infidelity without limits.' Circulated in Hebrew and Yiddish. This is distinct from the separately issued Haredi Rabbinical Council kol koreh (already in corpus) and the later Lakewood kol koreh.

detail.ab: SHRM's guide to ethical, efficient, and employee-centric AI integration published in late 2023. The playbook emphasizes a human-centered approach: 'To flourish in the AI era, we must rethink work, worker, and workplace — reimagining a world of work where human intelligence and ingenuity are upskilled, not replaced.' Addresses ethical considerations, transparency, training for responsible AI integration, and organizational synergy between AI and human intelligence (HI). Calls for human oversight in critical employment decisions including hiring and firing, and establishes the framework for SHRM's broader 'AI+HI' initiative. Includes guidance on governance frameworks for algorithmic fairness, data privacy, and ethical use.

detail.ab: A coalition letter from 26 organizations to Senate Majority Leader Schumer demanding Congress prioritize AI worker protections. Signed by Turkopticon, DAIR, Alphabet Workers Union-CWA, Amazon Employees for Climate Justice, AI Now Institute, and others. Key demands include: prioritizing health, safety, and fair wages for data workers developing AI; strengthening workers' organizing rights without employer surveillance; ending predatory workplace monitoring and automated management systems; addressing discriminatory 'black box' hiring, firing, and wage processes; and establishing transparency, due process, and job security protections.

detail.ab: Formal speech by Sheikh Abdullah bin Bayyah, Chairman of the UAE Council for Fatwa, delivered at the AI Ethics Forum in Rome on January 10, 2023. Emphasizes that Islamic teachings encourage knowledge acquisition while maintaining ethical frameworks. Argues AI innovations must be guided by moral principles to protect human dignity. Advocates an ethical framework based on universal values: virtue, respect for humanity, solidarity, cooperation, honesty, justice, integrity, accountability, and transparency. Invokes the Islamic legal maxim 'la darar wa la dirar' (there is no harm nor reciprocal harm) as a foundational principle for AI ethics. References UAE government vision and founding principles of Sheikh Zayed.

detail.ab: Independent, bipartisan report by the US Chamber's Commission on AI Competitiveness, Inclusion, and Innovation. Developed through 87 expert witness hearings across five field sessions. Establishes five foundational pillars for AI regulation: Efficiency (leverage existing laws, fill gaps), Collegiality (federal interagency coordination), Neutrality (technology-neutral, outcomes-focused rules), Flexibility (support industry self-regulation and soft-law approaches), and Proportionality (risk-based regulatory frameworks). Addresses workforce preparation, global competitiveness, and national security dimensions of AI policy.

detail.ab: Published September 18, 2023, with accompanying formal Policy Position Paper on AI. Five principles: (1) Creators' choice — creators should decide if and how they want their talent used; (2) Record keeping — tech providers must maintain auditable records of music ingested; (3) Human creativity — without human creativity there should be no copyright; (4) Labelling — AI-generated music must be labelled as such; (5) Protection of personality rights — a new personality right should protect the personality/image of songwriters and artists. Members include BPI, Musicians' Union, Music Publishers Association, PRS for Music, and others. Represents the collective position of the UK music industry including the Musicians' Union.

detail.ab: Regulatory comments submitted to the White House Office of Management and Budget on draft AI governance guidance. UnidosUS articulates three interconnected pillars for AI governance: Values (strong ethical guardrails against algorithmic bias), Voice (formal community engagement roles for impacted groups, especially Latino communities), and Investment (public capacity building for inclusive participation in technology governance). The organization warns that AI technologies carry inherent risks of scaling existing inequalities and that safeguards for civil liberties must accompany broader inclusive participation measures.

detail.ab: Unite the Union's guide and model workplace agreement on AI provides a framework for union officers and representatives. Key provisions: nothing introduced without union agreement; full training for union representatives before any AI/AMS consideration; human contact mandatory for workplace decisions (no automated detriment without human review); transparency requirements; union access to relevant data. Unite holds that AI benefits society only if linked to strong worker/citizen protections and effective collective bargaining.

detail.ab: By Emily Black, Logan Koepke, Pauline Kim, Solon Barocas, and Mingwei Hsu. Argues that for any given algorithmic prediction task there exist multiple models of equivalent predictive performance but divergent demographic impact, and that disparate impact law should require organizations deploying AI in civil rights domains (housing, employment, credit) to actively search for and implement less discriminatory alternatives before deployment.

detail.ab: Official JW.org educational article in the 'More Topics' series addressing AI from a biblical perspective. Acknowledges AI's dual nature: potential healthcare benefits vs. threats to safety, civil rights, privacy, and democratic institutions. Cites Proverbs 14:12 (human limitation in foreseeing consequences), Ecclesiastes 2:18-19 (loss of control over creations), and divine promises of protection. Takes balanced approach noting both risks and potential benefits while directing readers to biblical guidance rather than specific AI policy recommendations.

detail.ab: A technical policy brief arguing that trustworthiness mechanisms and measures advanced in AI regulations and standards may not actually increase public trust in AI. Contends that AI must not only be engineered to be trustworthy but must actually be trusted, and that fostering trust requires policymakers to demonstrate accountability to public concerns. Co-authored by John T. Richards (IBM) and Bran Knowles (Lancaster University). Published as ACM TechBrief Issue 9.

detail.ab: Formal institutional policy by ACS Publications governing the responsible use of AI tools in scientific publishing, last updated December 13, 2024. Establishes binding expectations for authors, reviewers, and editors across ACS journals. Key elements: authors must disclose all AI tool usage in the Acknowledgment section; AI tools cannot qualify for authorship; AI-generated content must be disclosed with description of when and how tools were used; reviewers are prohibited from disclosing manuscripts to third-party AI services (breach of confidentiality); editors may reject submissions with 'too extensive' AI use for substantive commentary. Grounded in existing ACS Ethical Guidelines to Publication of Chemical Research.

Demands legally binding mechanisms for AI regulation, mandatory employer consultation before AI introduction, transparency, privacy and anti-discrimination protections, and enforcement of consultation obligations. 'Nothing about us, without us.' Workers must be involved in every debate about AI.
